How Could It Hurt?: Scope creep; the Aug 2026 Next Chapter demo slips; solo-builder burnout
Existing Controls: Written design spec and phased plan with explicit non-goals; Phase 1 (workspace, Module 1, CLI, TUI, simulator slice) already built and passing 24 tests; milestones tracked in-repo
Additional Mitigation: Freeze the demo scope to Phase 1; defer the web GUI, full DOOM simulator, and the M6-M7 open-source track to later phases; validate estimates against the built slice

Risk: Facility review blocks the software from running inside
How Could It Hurt?: The primary distribution channel (inside the wall) closes; the core audience is unreachable
Existing Controls: Offline by design: one static-musl binary, no network or sockets, no crypto, assets embedded at compile time; a vocab-lint CI gate rejects hacking/exploitation language; targets the walled-garden laptops facilities already deploy
Additional Mitigation: Commission an early review by a DOC/facility contact; ship a plain-language security and review packet with a threat model; run a single-facility pilot before scaling

Risk: The DOOM finale reads as a game and trips facility review
How Could It Hurt?: A playable game is treated as entertainment or contraband and the whole course is rejected on that basis
Existing Controls: The finale is framed and presented as an engineering demonstration - running a real program on a display driven from the bare metal up, a technical capstone artifact, not entertainment
Additional Mitigation: Offer a non-game bootable demo (a graphics or test pattern) as an alternate finale for strict facilities; make the finale configurable; document the engineering framing in the review packet

Risk: Weak or misjudged labor-market demand for the skills (market realism)
How Could It Hurt?: Graduates cannot convert systems and Rust skills into work; the program's core promise fails
Existing Controls: Targets a verified underserved niche (no reentry program teaches systems; embedded and systems roles are short-staffed and pay more than saturated, shrinking web work); outcomes are portable public artifacts that signal skill across many roles, not only kernel jobs (83% of orgs weigh open-source contributions)
Additional Mitigation: Position outcomes for a range of roles (embedded, systems, tooling, QA, support), not only kernel work; set honest expectations - differentiation, not a guaranteed kernel job; track graduate placement in a pilot

Risk: Simulator cannot convincingly carry a learner to the DOOM finale
How Could It Hurt?: The emotional payoff and the program's core differentiator weaken; costly late rework
Existing Controls: First simulator slice already built and tested (modeled SPI bus + RGB565 panel + boot splash driven over the bus); an SpiBus trait provides the sim-or-real seam; doomgeneric is a proven-portable DOOM backend
Additional Mitigation: Build a DOOM-on-simulated-panel vertical slice early to de-risk it; keep a simpler bootable graphical demo as a fallback finale

Risk: Insufficient testing or inaccurate curriculum content
How Could It Hurt?: Learners are taught something wrong; credibility and trust are lost; support burden rises
Existing Controls: Unit tests across the workspace (24 passing); a self-validating content loader (tests assert every check's canonical answer grades correct); CI runs fmt, clippy with warnings denied, tests, and vocab-lint
Additional Mitigation: Mentor and subject-expert review of every lesson before release; grow coverage on the simulator and content paths; user-acceptance testing with a pilot learner

Risk: Learners get discouraged and drop out (retention)
How Could It Hurt?: Low completion undercuts outcomes and the whole case for the program
Existing Controls: Self-paced and mastery-based with no failing grade; the course cannot get stuck (help always on screen, no dead ends); an early visible payoff and a tracked checks-passed indicator
Additional Mitigation: A small peer cohort plus a mentor who has walked the path; sequence tiny early wins before hard material; measure completion in a pilot and iterate

Risk: Built but not adopted - no facility or organization deploys it
How Could It Hurt?: The course clears review and is free, yet never reaches learners; the effort is wasted
Existing Controls: Next Chapter as first cohort and champion; free with zero server or infrastructure lowers the adoption cost; positioned to complement, not compete with, existing programs
Additional Mitigation: Land one lighthouse facility pilot and publish its outcomes; partner with an organization that already has facility delivery rails; keep onboarding to a single copied binary

Risk: Privacy breach of a vulnerable population's data
How Could It Hurt?: Exposed progress, answers, or typed input could harm a learner's standing inside or on release
Existing Controls: Offline and local-only: no accounts, no network, no telemetry, no tracking; minimal data (a local progress file); nothing leaves the machine
Additional Mitigation: Per-user state isolation and an easy wipe on shared machines; store no more free text than a check needs; document data handling for facility review; least-privilege file access

Risk: Open-source first-patch step overwhelms beginners or yields low-quality patches
How Could It Hurt?: Discouragement at the most important moment; wasted maintainer time and reputational cost
Existing Controls: Scope kept small to start (docs and staging-tree fixes); plugs into established on-ramps (kernelnewbies, Linux Kernel Mentorship Program, Outreachy); meritocracy framing separates code critique from the person
Additional Mitigation: Internal mentor review of every patch before it is sent upstream; a curated, scaffolded first-issue list; pair each learner with a buddy for their first submission

Risk: No sponsor or funding to sustain the program or equip open-build learners
How Could It Hurt?: Hardware is out of reach for outside learners; long-term sustainability is at risk
Existing Controls: Free and open source (MIT / Apache-2.0); simulator-first design means the full course runs with zero hardware and zero cost; the $200 Saleae writeup bounty is available now
Additional Mitigation: Apply for education and reentry grants; pursue Saleae and other hardware-partner outreach; keep every learning outcome reachable with no hardware at all

Risk: Curriculum drifts out of date as the kernel and Rust evolve
How Could It Hurt?: Version-specific claims (kernel state, the Rust SPI gap) age; content loses accuracy and credibility
Existing Controls: Time-sensitive claims are date-stamped and sourced; content is plain markdown, cheap to revise; the core concepts (syscalls, mmap, SPI) are stable and long-lived
Additional Mitigation: A review cadence on version-specific claims; keep current-state facts in one place, separate from durable concepts; re-verify the kernel and Rust landscape each cycle

Risk: The market gap closes or the technical differentiator is overtaken
How Could It Hurt?: An existing program adds systems, or the Rust SPI abstraction lands upstream, eroding the first-or-novel claim
Existing Controls: The differentiator is verified and date-stamped; the durable value (a portable portfolio and a public contribution record) survives even if the niche gets crowded; first-mover plus lived experience is defensible
Additional Mitigation: Re-verify the landscape each cycle; lead with the durable value, not novelty; if the Rust SPI abstraction lands upstream, pivot the capstone to the next unfilled area such as GPIO

Risk: Dependency on external communities and the host organization
How Could It Hurt?: Maintainers reject the cohort's patches, or a partner deprioritizes; the payoff or reach weakens
Existing Controls: Start with low-bar, high-acceptance contributions (docs, staging); multiple on-ramps rather than one; mentor pre-review raises acceptance odds; the core course stands alone without any single partner
Additional Mitigation: Cultivate a friendly-maintainer and first-issue list; diversify host and partner relationships; keep the curriculum independent of any one community

How Could It Hurt?: Knowledge loss; the project stalls; the lived-experience voice is lost
Existing Controls: All specs, plans, curriculum, and code are open source and documented in-repo (PRD, design spec, phase plan, README); CI encodes the quality bar as executable knowledge
Additional Mitigation: Grow co-maintainers out of the peer community; write an onboarding and contributor guide; identify a backup content reviewer
